narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam as riskier than we'd hoped—landing at a 60% chance of not passing. Picture this as your story unfolding through the lens of patterns from thousands of students like you: your Year 3 grades placing you in the bottom decile of your school stood out as the biggest signal, whispering that the rigors of legal studies might prove an uphill battle even if you poured your heart into it. That low decile1 from Year 1 echoed it loudly too, suggesting those early academic hurdles set a tougher foundation than most who make it through. Your LSAT score of 23 then piled on, as it often marks a steep climb for the intense bar prep ahead, pulling the prediction firmly toward challenge. Layered with your lower-middle family income, which can limit access to the tutors or resources that smooth the way for others, and your undergraduate GPA of 3.2, which while solid didn't quite shine against the top performers who typically pass. Being a full-time Hispanic woman added subtle strains too—stats show these intersections sometimes mean facing extra barriers in high-stakes exams like the bar, though your determination shines through regardless. Together, these pieces painted a picture of a resilient fighter whose profile matches many who've stumbled at the finish line, not because of who you are, but because the data sees echoes of those patterns. It's not the end of your story—this is just one model's view, and with targeted steps like LSAT retakes or extra study support, you could rewrite it. You've got the fire; now channel it wisely. Keep pushing.
